[MORPHOLOGY]

- Affixation direction varies by language: suffix-strong in Marathi (Indo-European), Klamath (Penutian), Telugu (Dravidian), Hungarian (Uralic), Nama (Khoe-Kwadi); prefix-weak in Kalispel (Salishan); suffix-weak in Komo (Koman) and Bilua (Solomons East Papuan); equal prefix/suffix in Choctaw (Muskogean). The morphology transformation matrix must account for affixation direction or lose grammatical nuance.
- Bilua (Solomons East Papuan) shows weak suffixing while Choctaw (Muskogean) has equal prefixing and suffixing; systems must adjust processing per affixation preference to prevent morphological mismatches. (symbol: [BLU]≫morph:suffix-weak, [CHO]≫morph:pref-suf-equal)

[MORPHOLOGY]

- Kanashi, Baragaunle, Kachari, and Newari (Sino-Tibetan) express negation purely morphologically via affixes or internal changes rather than independent negative words; systems relying on independent negation tokens will fail to recognize or generate negation, so tokenization must account for morphological negation. (symbol: [KAN]≫neg:morph, [BAR]≫neg:morph, [KAC]≫neg:morph, [NEW]≫neg:morph)
[SYNTAX]
- Negation placement is not predictable from word order alone: Japanese has no preverbal negative morpheme; Tutelo and Tirmaga require obligatory double negation while Kiliwa's double negation is optional; Baragaunle/Newari negate morphologically inside the verb; Warluwara uses NegSOV; Orokaiva/Kaluli allow both Neg-V and V-Neg orders. Misalignment causes scope inversion, ungrammatical clauses, or morphology destruction. (symbol: [JP]≫neg:none)
yae:
[SYNTAX]
- Negation patterns vary structurally beyond mere placement: Inanwatan allows optional double negation, Russian uses predicate negation alongside negative indefinite pronouns, Yaruro uses symmetric standard negation, and Japanese lacks preverbal negative morphemes. Incorrect negation structure risks contradictory meanings. (symbol: [IN]≫syntax:optional-double-neg, [RU]≫syntax:pred-negation, [YAR]≫syntax:symmetric-negation, [JP]≫morph:no-preverbal-neg)
- Additional negation-placement patterns: symmetric negation in Yaruro; predicate negation in Maybrat; double negation in Tirmaga (Eastern Sudanic); verb-initial/preverbal in Squamish (Salishan); multiple positions in Batak Karo (Austronesian); SVONeg in Tiv (Niger-Congo); SNegVO in Nahuatl Michoacán (Uto-Aztecan) — translation engines must recognize each specific pattern rather than defaulting to common family patterns.
yao:
[SYNTAX]
- Direct word-order copying between families (SOV↔SVO, V2 vs V-final, SOV↔VSO, topic-prominent vs subject-prominent, ergative vs nominative-accusative, postposition vs preposition) causes subject/object reversal or clause fragmentation; some languages (Dhargari, Suki, Hmar, Soninke) deviate from their family's expected default order and need individual handling. (symbol: [UT]≈order:reorder-clause)
- VO order confirmed in Tiwi, Yao, and Tepehua/Huehuetla across diverse families; Western Armenian shows OV as a notable exception within Indo-European; Kasong adds SV documentation; Kutenai confirms VS as distinct pattern; Yimas lacks a dominant word order, creating structural ambiguity requiring special resolution strategies. (symbol: [TIW|YAO|TEP]≫word-order:VO, [ARM]≫word-order:OV, [KAS]≫word-order:SV, [YIM]≫word-order:no-dominant)
ycn:
[SYNTAX]
- Possessives are typically Noun-Genitive (Swahili, Armenian) with exception Genitive-Noun (Konni); numerals are typically Noun-Numeral (Awyi, Dhaasanac, Epena Pedee) with exception Numeral-Noun (Taiof); adjectives vary between Noun-Adjective (Guajajara, Tamabo) and Adjective-Noun (Wakhi); demonstratives typically Demonstrative-Noun (Yucuna) with exception Noun-Demonstrative (Simeulue); adpositions are prepositional in Indo-European (Ayiwo, Iban) but postpositional in Uralic (Udmurt). (symbol: [NGen]≠[KON:GenN], [NUM]≠[TAIO:N-N], [AP]≠[UDM:post])
- Simeulue places demonstratives after the noun (Noun-Demonstrative), while Yucuna and Maru place them before (Demonstrative-Noun); misalignment confuses referent interpretation. (symbol: [DEM]≫noun-first, [DEM]≫dem-first)
- VO languages typically use prepositions (Wolof, Sundanese, Russian, Linda) while OV languages use postpositions (Kwoma-Sepik, Ladakhi-Sino-Tibetan), but Yucuna (Arawakan) is a VO exception using postpositions; adposition placement must be adjusted per target language to avoid noun-phrase mistranslation. (symbol: [Adposition]≫[VO/OV](LANGUAGE-ORDER))
- Guajajara (Tupian) is a VO language using postpositions, extending the exception pattern beyond Yucuna (Arawakan); postposition reordering is required despite VO structure. (symbol: [VO]≫[Postpositions](TUPIAN/ARAWAKAN))
yee:
[SYNTAX]
- VO order confirmed in Tiwi, Yao, and Tepehua/Huehuetla across diverse families; Western Armenian shows OV as a notable exception within Indo-European; Kasong adds SV documentation; Kutenai confirms VS as distinct pattern; Yimas lacks a dominant word order, creating structural ambiguity requiring special resolution strategies. (symbol: [TIW|YAO|TEP]≫word-order:VO, [ARM]≫word-order:OV, [KAS]≫word-order:SV, [YIM]≫word-order:no-dominant)
[TYPOLOGY]
- Documents word-order exceptions (OV: Armenian, Ika, Hopi; VO: Tiwi; SV: Sema, Wakhi, Kasong; VS: Kutenai; non-dominant: Yimas; Noun-Numeral: Dhaasanac, Awyi), negation (NegV: Konni, Runyankore, /Xam), possession (Noun-Genitive: Iban; Genitive-Noun: Koromfe), ditransitives (indirect-object: Nias, Udmurt), suffix negation without doubling (Mian), absence of 2nd-person imperatives (Skou), optative mood (Ladakhi), Noun-Adjective order (Guajajara, Cocama), and prepositions (Ayiwo).
ygr:
[PHONOLOGY]
- Tone loss in Mandarin and Yagaria causes roughly 30% meaning ambiguity; phonemic vowel length (Urdu, Japanese) versus non-phonemic Indo-European vowel length, and nasalized-vowel approximation, both risk further lexical ambiguity, requiring tone maps or probability markers to be preserved. (symbol: [TONE]≫loss:semantic-30%)
- Yagaria uses a simple tone system for lexical distinction where tone loss creates roughly 30% ambiguity; tone-preservation protocols are required when translating between tonal and non-tonal languages to prevent meaning loss.
yor:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Bantu noun-class agreement (obligatory on verbs and adjectives, e.g. Zulu, Swahili) has no equivalent in Indo-European; mitigation is to tag nouns with CLASS_TAG and propagate it to modifiers, inserting a pronoun if the target lacks class marking. Kirghiz and Gooniyandi lack grammatical gender entirely, and Grebo's gender system is not sex-based, so gendered-pronoun assumptions misrepresent subjects. (symbol: CLASS_TAG)
- Yoruba (Niger-Congo) uses a pure vigesimal (base-20) numeral system; direct decimal conversion misrepresents quantities and requires base conversion or annotation, since most other families use base-10 or mixed systems. (symbol: [YO]≫num:base20)
[PHONOLOGY]
- Lexical and grammatical tone (e.g. Mandarin's 4-tone system, Yoruba aspect-marking tone, Igbo High/Low) causes roughly 30% ambiguity when translated to atonal Indo-European languages unless explicitly tagged (e.g. #H/#L) and propagated through the DOT so it can be re-inserted as pitch-contour cues in TTS/STT.
- Specific missing phonemes (Japanese /ɸ/, Dravidian retroflex /ɭ/, Turkic /ɲ/, West-African /ɡ͡b/, Afro-Asiatic pharyngeals, Khoisan clicks) have defined substitution targets (e.g. /ɸ/→/f/, /ɭ/→/l/); phonotactic mismatches (Mandarin disallows consonant clusters, Japanese moraic constraints) require epenthesis or gemination when importing loanwords.
[SYNTAX]
- Negation placement in SOV languages varies: NegSOV occurs in Mono (Uto-Aztecan), Yoruba (Niger-Congo), and Sumerian (isolate); SONegV occurs in Mangghuer (Altaic); SO[Neg-V] occurs in Pashto (Indo-European), with further variability in Anufo, Ncàm, and Logoti — systems must adapt to language-specific negation rules rather than assuming a family-level default.
yul:
[SYNTAX]
- Negation placement is not predictable from word order alone: Japanese has no preverbal negative morpheme; Tutelo and Tirmaga require obligatory double negation while Kiliwa's double negation is optional; Baragaunle/Newari negate morphologically inside the verb; Warluwara uses NegSOV; Orokaiva/Kaluli allow both Neg-V and V-Neg orders. Misalignment causes scope inversion, ungrammatical clauses, or morphology destruction. (symbol: [JP]≫neg:none)
- Yulu uses an SVNegO negation order, deviating from standard preverbal/initial negation placement and potentially causing misalignment with translations expecting standard SVO structures.
- Negation placement correlates with word order but has exceptions: SOV languages like Pashto and Assamese place negation pre-verbally, SVO Nahuatl also uses pre-verb negation, Mangghuer (Altaic) uses mid-sentence negation, and Warluwara (Pama-Nyungan) and Yulu (Central Sudanic, SV Neg O) deviate from the norm; assuming universal post-verb negation causes clause misordering and semantic reversal. (symbol: [PAS]≫neg:preverb_SOV, [ASS]≫neg:preverb_SOV, [NAH]≫neg:preverb_SVO, [MNG]≫neg:mid_SOV, [WAR]≫neg:SOV_exception)
zap:
[HAZARD]
- Tutelo requires obligatory double negation; Kiliwa has irregular negative constructions; Orokaiva uses multiple negative constructions in SOV order; Newari has irregular negation position; Xhosa uses VO structure with Noun-Relative alignment; Zapotec uses VSO order. Unmapped negation or syntax patterns cause semantic confusion or structural inaccuracy. (symbol: [TU]≫negation:obligatory-double, [KI]≫negation:irregular-structure, [OR]≫negation:multiple-construct, [NE]≫negation:irregular-position, [XO]≫syntax:VO-NRel, [ZP]≫syntax:VSO)
[SYNTAX]
- Interrogative-phrase positioning varies: Nahuatl and Zapotec place interrogatives initially, Mien places them later in the sentence, and Gumuz lacks polar question particles entirely, requiring sentence restructuring to signal a question. (symbol: [AUST]≫interrog:init)
zul:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Bantu noun-class agreement (obligatory on verbs and adjectives, e.g. Zulu, Swahili) has no equivalent in Indo-European; mitigation is to tag nouns with CLASS_TAG and propagate it to modifiers, inserting a pronoun if the target lacks class marking. Kirghiz and Gooniyandi lack grammatical gender entirely, and Grebo's gender system is not sex-based, so gendered-pronoun assumptions misrepresent subjects. (symbol: CLASS_TAG)
[SYNTAX]
- Direct word-order copying between families (SOV↔SVO, V2 vs V-final, SOV↔VSO, topic-prominent vs subject-prominent, ergative vs nominative-accusative, postposition vs preposition) causes subject/object reversal or clause fragmentation; some languages (Dhargari, Suki, Hmar, Soninke) deviate from their family's expected default order and need individual handling. (symbol: [UT]≈order:reorder-clause)
cnl:
[SYNTAX]
- Chinantec (Lealao dialect, Oto-Manguean family) is a VO-order language. Verbal argument structure must be mapped explicitly when translating to/from OV-order languages to avoid argument-structure translation inaccuracies. (symbol: [ObjOrd]≠VO)
kal:
[DATA QUALITY]
- Greenlandic shares the 2-letter compression symbol [GR] with Grebo in isolate outputs. DOT lookups and downstream systems must disambiguate by full ISO 639-3 code (kal vs grb), not the shared symbol, to avoid cross-language knowledge bleed.
arr:
[SYNTAX]
- Karó (Tupian family) places the genitive modifier before the head noun (Genitive-Noun order), unlike Noun-Genitive languages such as Gurung, Jingpho, Khasi, and Kamba. Incorrect placement risks misinterpreting possessive relationships — critical for relational meaning accuracy. (symbol: [GenOrd]≠GenN)
lbu:
[SYNTAX]
- Labu is a VO-order language and follows Noun-Adjective modifier order (shared with Tausug and Wolof). Assuming Adjective-Noun order for Labu scrambles modifier relations in translated output.
mri:
[PHONOLOGY]
- Maori is a CV-only (consonant-vowel) language. Complex consonant clusters carried over from source languages (e.g. Polish "szcz") are phonotactically incompatible and require epenthesis with a default vowel (/ə/); flag CLUSTER_GAP when this substitution occurs.
myn:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Mayan languages use graded pronoun suffixes marking actor identity (self vs. stranger) rather than the subjective/objective pronoun collapse typical of Indo-European languages. Translating into Mayan requires an actor-relationship distinction the source text may not encode; omitting it risks losing agency specificity in contexts like legal or human-rights text. (symbol: [MY]⊕morph:suffix-gender-ingredient)
ppo:
[DATA QUALITY]
- Paamese shares the 2-letter compression symbol [PA] with Paiute (and with Paumarí, under a separate tagging pass) in isolate outputs. DOT lookups must disambiguate by full ISO 639-3 code (ppo), not the shared symbol.
pao:
[DATA QUALITY]
- Paiute shares the 2-letter compression symbol [PA] with Paamese (and with Paumarí, under a separate tagging pass) in isolate outputs. DOT lookups must disambiguate by full ISO 639-3 code (pao), not the shared symbol.
pad:
[TYPOLOGY]
- Paumarí (Arauan family) is SOV-order and agglutinative. Its plosive consonant series has complete voicing (voiced/voiceless pairs for every plosive) — do not assume a partial or default voicing pattern when mapping its phonology to or from a target language. (symbol: [PA]≫plosive:voicing:complete)
[DATA QUALITY]
- Paumarí's compression symbol [PA] collides with Paamese and Paiute under a separate tagging pass in this corpus — disambiguate by full ISO 639-3 code (pad), not the shared symbol.
myp:
[SEMANTICS]
- Pirahã has a limited color-term system relative to Indo-European norms. This complicates establishing precise color references and can increase ambiguity when descriptive/identifying language depends on fine-grained color distinctions. (symbol: [PRH]≫color:limited-system)
tsg:
[SYNTAX]
- Tausug is a VO-order language and follows Noun-Adjective modifier order (shared with Labu and Wolof). Assuming Adjective-Noun order for Tausug scrambles modifier relations in translated output.
mzh:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Wichí (Matacoan family) has no morphological case-marking at all. Grammatical relations rely entirely on word order and context rather than case cues. Translating into case-sensitive target languages (e.g. Hungarian) risks loss of relational clarity between sentence elements; conversely, case information present in a case-marking source language has no morphological slot to map onto when translating into Wichí — it must be re-expressed via word order/context instead. (symbol: [MA]≫case:none, [MA]≫case:zero-marking)
bod:
[TYPOLOGY]
- Tibetan (Modern Literary) is Sino-Tibetan, SOV word order, agglutinative morphology, and atonal (no lexical tone) — do not assume the tonal patterns common elsewhere in the Sino-Tibetan family apply here. (symbol: [TB]=Tibetan (Modern Literary) (Sino-Tibetan · SOV · AGG · ATONE))
ita:
[SYNTAX]
- Italian follows Degree-Adjective modifier order (the degree word precedes the adjective, e.g. "molto bello"), shared with Marathi and Gulf Arabic. Assuming Adjective-Degree order (as in Bimoba, Jur Mödö, Cham) produces unnatural phrasing in translated output.
deu:
[SYNTAX]
- German exhibits a dominant Noun-Relative (N-Rel) clause structure in relativization. Assuming a different relative-clause order scrambles referent interpretation.
[MORPHOLOGY]
- German has a grammatical gender system (unlike genderless languages such as Khalaj or Georgian) — noun/adjective agreement must track gender, not be stripped or ignored when translating from a genderless source language. (symbol: gender:present)
abk:
[SYNTAX]
- Multiple negative constructions. Variability in negative constructions could lead to inaccuracies in translation of negative meanings. (symbol: multi-neg-constructions)
abt:
[SYNTAX]
- SOV language with multiple syntactically distinct negative constructions (syntax) whose selection is pragmatically conditioned (pragmatics); translation lacks context to choose the correct negation slot. A single source negative sentence maps to several target structures (e.g., Neg-S-O-V, S-Neg-O-V, S-O-V-Neg); without discourse features (focus, polarity, tense), the system guesses, yielding pragmatic infelicities. (symbol: Pragmatic negation choice)
aca:
[SYNTAX]
- VO basic order combined with postpositional adpositions (an atypical typological pairing). Automatic translation pipelines that assume VO languages use prepositions will mis‑place relational phrases, producing unintelligible or semantically distorted output. (symbol: VOpost mismatch hazard)
ace:
[SYNTAX]
- Unpredictable adposition-noun phrase order. Potential ambiguity in translation due to unclear adposition placement. (symbol: adp:unpredict)
acm:
[SYNTAX]
- adposition‑order‑non‑harmonic. Using the universal OV/postposition or VO/preposition rule will place adpositions incorrectly for Acoma, leading to grammatically malformed noun phrases. (symbol: adposition order other)
adn:
[SYNTAX]
- relclause‑order‑non‑harmonic. Automatic mapping to the standard OV‑NRel pattern will mis‑order relative clauses, causing comprehension errors in the translated output. (symbol: relative clause other)
ady:
[SYNTAX]
- Adposition-noun phrase order does not correlate with verb-object typology (non-canonical). Automated adposition placement (pre- vs post-position) based on OV status fails; requires explicit lookup. (symbol: OV>>adpos-order-unpredictable)
adz:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Relative clause positioning issues. Relative clauses may be misinterpreted or incorrectly placed in translation. (symbol: [XX]>>VO-NRel)
aes:
[SYNTAX]
- VO basic order but adjectives precede nouns (AdjN), contravening the typical VO → NAdj correlation; noun-phrase internal order is head-initial for verb/object but head-final for noun/adjective. Statistical MT and rule-based NP generators default to NAdj for VO languages, producing *[N Adj]* instead of grammatical *[Adj N]*; this error cascades in DP agreement and demonstrative placement. (symbol: VO prenominal Adj)
agb:
[SYNTAX]
- OV clause order combined with Adjective-Noun (AdjN) ordering. The source’s adjective‑noun order conflicts with many target languages’ noun‑adjective preference, risking semantic distortion. (symbol: agb>>adj:order-mismatch)
agt:
[SYNTAX]
- VO order with AdjN affects complement placement. Misordered translations with modifiers. (symbol: VO>>AdjN)
ajg:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Multiple negative constructions collapse to a single target construction, losing variation in negative placement. FEATURE: Variable placement of the negative morpheme in SVO clauses clashes with target languages that have a single, fixed negative position. Nuances conveyed by different negative placements (e.g., scope, emphasis) are lost. The chosen negative construction may not align with the target’s syntax, causing loss of negation scope or unnatural word order. (symbol: neg placement ambiguous)
alh:
[SYNTAX]
- Other ordering patterns in relative clauses. Non-standard structures may create translation challenges in clarity and meaning. (symbol: [XX]>>unordered-relatives)
aly:
[SYNTAX]
- Relative-Clause Order – NRel. The placement of relative clauses impacts translation into languages with differing syntactic structures, possibly leading to ambiguity. (symbol: NREL-ORDER)
amk:
[SYNTAX]
- Basic Word Order – Verb-Object (VO). The standard Verb-Object structure may conflict with languages favoring Object-Verb order, leading to structural inconsistencies in translation. (symbol: VO-ORDER)
amo:
[SYNTAX]
- VO order with NAdj ordering. incorrect adjective placement in translation can obscure meaning. (symbol: VO-NAdj)
ani:
[SYNTAX]
- Person marker omission. Loss of agent-patient relationships in translated sentences, leading to ambiguity in roles. (symbol: marker:omission)
any:
[SYNTAX]

apy:
[SYNTAX]
- Negative word position variability. Ambiguity and confusion in the translation of negation leading to misleading meanings. (symbol: neg:position:variability)
aqc:
[SYNTAX]

arn:
[SYNTAX]

aro:

arz:
[MORPHOLOGY]

- Inconsistent marking patterns. Ambiguities in sentence structure risk misinterpretation in translation. (symbol: [XX]>>marking-inconsistent)
asm:
[SYNTAX]
- Inconsistent / “other” locus of marking (no clear head‑ or dependent‑marking system). Morphosyntactic alignment cannot be reliably inferred, so case‑mapping or agreement generation in translation will be erroneous or omitted. (symbol: Marking inconsistent hazard)
ava:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- OV order with postpositions. post‑position to pre‑position conversion required for target languages; otherwise PP semantics are lost. (symbol: OV post‑pos)
avu:
[SYNTAX]
- Clause order and relative-clause order both "Other" eliminates typological anchors for constituent sequencing in main and embedded clauses, creating uncertainty about relative-clause placement. Both main clause linearization and RC-noun ordering unconstrained by typology; high risk of word-order errors. Translators may attach relative clauses to the wrong noun, producing mis-referencing or dangling modifiers. (symbol: [avu]>>ord:other-dbl)
ayz:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Verbs encode at most one inflectional category (0–1 per word), yielding minimal synthesis where TAM, person, and number are largely absent from the verb complex. Translation into moderate/high synthesis languages requires expanding each verb into a fully inflected form using only clause-external context; zero-marked categories (e.g., tense, subject) become guesswork without discourse anchors, risking omission of crucial TAM/person information. (symbol: ayz>>synth:minimal)
azb:
[SYNTAX]

bbc:
[SYNTAX]

bej:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- verb‑synthesis‑argument‑loss. When translating to a language with low‑synthesis morphology, the bundled subject/object information encoded in a single verb may be omitted or mis‑assigned, resulting in ambiguous or incorrect clause‑level meaning. (symbol: verb synthesis loss)
bfd:

bft:

bgr:

blc:
[SYNTAX]
- V-initial negation barred from post-verbal and S-O-intervening focus positions, requiring alternative focus strategies. Focused negation ("NOT go") cannot use default focus position; must use clefting, pre-verbal negation, or particles, restructuring clause. (symbol: [blc]>>neg:focus-blocked)
brb:

brc:

bri:

(symbol: [bsk]>>evidential:verb-fused)
btx:

bza:
[SYNTAX]

caq:
[SYNTAX]

cay:
[SYNTAX]

cco:
[SYNTAX]

cha:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Inconsistent locus of marking forces non‑deterministic mapping; translation must flag and may preserve source marking, risking target grammar errors. FEATURE: Inconsistent locus‑of‑marking makes grammatical‑role mapping unclear when the target language uses a uniform head‑ or dependent‑marking system. Argument structure may be misaligned; grammatical relations become unclear in the target. Translators may misinterpret subject/object relations, producing sentences with swapped arguments or missing case/agreement cues. (symbol: inconsistent marking ambiguity)
- SVO language with more than one construction for negative morpheme placement; specific positional semantics (emphasis, scope) are variable. Ambiguous negation placement in source risks incorrect scope/emphasis mapping in fixed-negation targets; translation systems enforcing a single negation slot may drop or mis-place the negative element. Exceptions: target languages that also exhibit flexible negation positioning (e.g., Turkish) reduce the hazard. (symbol: cha>>multi-neg-ambiguity)
chf:
[MORPHOLOGY]

Verb-bound agreement and role indices disappear in translation, requiring case/adposition insertion on arguments to preserve relational clarity. Translators may omit agent/patient roles because the target language expects case-marked nouns; resulting sentences can become ambiguous or unintelligible. (symbol: [chf]>>mark:head>dep)
chh:

chr:

cht:
